prompt:
Cleanup ticket on roster.ts, our newsletter-roster client. It's 2020-style promise chains, and every method resolves to a { ok, ... } result object that never rejects. In practice that's been a bug factory: three incidents this year were callers reading .data off a result they never checked, and TypeScript can't save them because everything is behind that union. We're standardizing on async/await plus thrown typed errors across the codebase, and roster.ts is next.

What I want in the same file: a RosterClient class constructed with the same injected transport (don't change the transport contract — the CLI and the tests hand one in), async methods getSubscriber(id), subscribe(email, name), unsubscribe(id). Success now resolves straight to the payload (unsubscribe resolves to null), failure THROWS. Error taxonomy: a RosterError base class with a .code, and subclasses NotFoundError, AlreadySubscribedError (carries .email), ValidationError (carries .fields), RateLimitError (carries .retryAfterSeconds), NetworkError for transport rejections (original error attached as .cause). Unexpected statuses throw the base RosterError itself with code 'api'. Each class sets .name to its class name.

Two things must not drift. First, the wire behavior: identical requests, identical status handling. Second, the error MESSAGE strings: they must be byte-identical to what the old result objects carried ('subscriber s_9 not found', 'rate limited, retry in 30s', etc.) because the on-call dashboards grep logs for those exact phrases. The result-object shape itself is dead — nothing should resolve with an ok flag ever again. test_roster.ts pins all of this; run node --test test_roster.ts and leave the test file alone.
